sections.title: Functions and Errors
sections.summary: Teach learners to shape behavior with functions, handle failure explicitly, and prepare for more structured project work.

sections.title: Types and Interfaces
sections.summary: Teach learners to model data with structs, attach behavior with methods, and rely on interfaces and generics to build reusable contracts.

sections.title: Composition
sections.summary: Teach learners to model has-a relationships with named fields, then use embedding and method promotion deliberately without confusing them for inheritance.

sections.title: Strings and Text
sections.summary: Teach learners how Go handles immutable strings, formatting, Unicode, regex, and template-driven text rendering for real parsing and reporting work.

sections.title: Modules and Packages
sections.summary: Teach learners how Go modules define package boundaries, dependency resolution, versioned imports, and optional build surfaces.

sections.title: I/O and CLI
sections.summary: Teach command-line input, JSON encoding and decoding, and filesystem work as one practical I/O section with multiple learner tracks.

sections.title: Web and Database
sections.summary: Teach the first production-shaped data-access surfaces through database/sql, transactions, and repository boundaries while keeping the broader web material available as legacy reference.

sections.title: Concurrency
sections.summary: Teach goroutines, context-based cancellation, and timer-driven workflows through three practical concurrency tracks while leaving deeper reference lessons available for later alpha work.

sections.title: Concurrency Patterns
sections.summary: Teach errgroup, context-driven cancellation, and deliberate sync.Pool usage through bounded concurrency exercises while leaving the worker-pool reference surface available for later alpha work.

sections.title: Quality and Performance
sections.summary: Teach the core testing and profiling habits that help Go engineers prove correctness and investigate performance before reaching for bigger architecture changes.

sections.title: Application Architecture
sections.summary: Teach the package, logging, and shutdown decisions that make Go services maintainable once they grow beyond a single file or a single happy path.

items.title: Functions
items.summary: Introduce basic function signatures, parameters, and return values.
objectives: Define small functions with clear inputs and outputs
Read parameter and return contracts without guessing hidden behavior
items.production_relevance: Small, explicit function contracts are the foundation for readable Go code and later error-return patterns.

items.title: Closures and Recursion
items.summary: Show how functions can capture state and call themselves when the problem shape needs it.
objectives: Explain how closures retain access to surrounding variables
Use recursion deliberately without losing track of the call flow
items.production_relevance: Closures and recursion appear in handlers, callbacks, and traversal logic, but they need disciplined readability to stay maintainable.

items.title: Variadic Functions
items.summary: Introduce variadic parameters and the spread form for passing slices into function calls.
objectives: Write functions that accept a flexible number of values
Recognize when variadic APIs improve clarity and when a slice is clearer
items.production_relevance: Variadic APIs are common in formatting, logging, and helpers, but they work best when the call shape stays obvious.

items.title: Multiple Return Values
items.summary: Teach Go's multi-return style and the path toward returning explicit errors as values.
objectives: Return multiple values from a function intentionally
Explain why Go pairs useful results with explicit error returns
items.production_relevance: Multiple return values are the base contract for idiomatic Go error handling and explicit control flow.

items.title: Custom Errors
items.summary: Show how to define custom error values that expose stable failure meaning.
objectives: Implement custom error types that carry useful context
Use stable error identity instead of fragile string matching
items.production_relevance: Stable custom errors make branching, retries, and user-safe handling easier across real Go services and tools.

items.title: Error Wrapping
items.summary: Teach how to wrap low-level failures with more context while preserving inspectable error chains.
objectives: Wrap errors with contextual information using idiomatic Go patterns
Inspect wrapped errors safely with the standard library helpers
items.production_relevance: Wrapped errors let logs, callers, and retry logic keep context without destroying the underlying failure identity.

items.title: Defer
items.summary: Introduce defer as a cleanup and completion tool that keeps function exits disciplined.
objectives: Use defer for cleanup and completion tracking
Explain when deferred work improves reliability and when it obscures intent
items.production_relevance: Defer is central to cleanup in file, lock, and transaction workflows where correctness depends on predictable exit behavior.

items.title: Panic and Recover
items.summary: Show where panic belongs, where it does not, and how recover works only at controlled defer boundaries.
objectives: Explain why panic is not a replacement for ordinary error returns
Recover only at deliberate safety boundaries inside deferred code
items.production_relevance: Reliable Go code treats panic as an exceptional boundary concern rather than everyday flow control, especially in servers and workers.

items.title: Error Handling Project
items.summary: Guide learners through combining custom errors, wrapped failures, and defer-based cleanup in one focused exercise.
objectives: Build a small program that applies the section's explicit error-handling ideas together
Preserve useful failure context by wrapping low-level errors without hiding the underlying math failure
items.production_relevance: Small tools and services often need to continue handling expected failures while still surfacing enough context for debugging and review.

items.title: Functional Options Pattern
items.summary: Introduce the functional options pattern as a scalable way to configure behavior without constructor explosion.
objectives: Explain how option functions separate defaults from configuration overrides
Recognize when the pattern helps and when it overcomplicates a small API
items.production_relevance: Functional options appear in many Go libraries where configuration flexibility matters, but they should be used intentionally rather than by habit.

items.title: Structs
items.summary: Introduce structs as Go's core data-modeling tool without inheritance or hidden class behavior.
objectives: Define structs that group related data into readable shapes
Explain when passing a struct by value copies data and when a pointer avoids that cost
items.production_relevance: Struct design is the basis for every later Go model, request payload, configuration shape, and domain type.

items.title: Methods
items.summary: Show how methods attach behavior to types and why receiver choice affects semantics and performance.
objectives: Define methods on concrete types with clear receiver intent
Explain the tradeoff between value receivers and pointer receivers
items.production_relevance: Receiver choice affects mutability, method sets, and copy costs in real Go APIs, not just toy examples.

items.title: Interfaces
items.summary: Teach interface contracts, implicit satisfaction, and how Go achieves polymorphism without inheritance.
objectives: Explain implicit interface satisfaction without relying on class-style inheritance
Use interfaces to describe behavior boundaries instead of concrete implementation details
items.production_relevance: Interfaces define seams for testing, package boundaries, and flexible architecture in production Go systems.

items.title: Stringer
items.summary: Use fmt.Stringer as the first standard-library interface integration point for custom types.
objectives: Implement fmt.Stringer to give custom values readable string output
Connect concrete type methods to a standard-library interface contract
items.production_relevance: Readable String methods improve logs, debug output, and CLI feedback without scattering formatting logic everywhere.

items.title: Generics
items.summary: Introduce type parameters and constraints as a way to write reusable, type-safe helpers without reflection.
objectives: Use type parameters and constraints to generalize helper logic safely
Recognize when generics improve reuse and when a concrete function is clearer
items.production_relevance: Generics help remove duplicate collection helpers and reusable utilities while preserving compile-time type safety.

items.title: Payroll Processor Project
items.summary: Combine structs, methods, interfaces, and a small generic helper into one polymorphic payroll exercise.
objectives: Build a small payroll domain that relies on interface contracts instead of inheritance
Show that multiple concrete employee types can be processed through one common behavior boundary
items.production_relevance: Real Go services often model multiple concrete types behind shared interfaces, especially in payment, billing, and reporting flows.

items.title: Advanced Generics
items.summary: Push generics further with reusable collection operators and a clearer understanding of type inference limits.
objectives: Use generic collection helpers like map and filter without losing type clarity
Explain where type inference helps and where generic APIs become too clever
items.production_relevance: Advanced generic helpers can reduce repetition in utility layers, but they only help when the resulting API stays readable for teams.

items.title: Composition
items.summary: Introduce named-field composition as Go's main way to build larger types from smaller focused components.
objectives: Explain the difference between has-a composition and class-style is-a inheritance
Access behavior through named component fields without hiding where the behavior comes from
items.production_relevance: Real Go systems rely on composition to keep domain types explicit, reusable, and easier to refactor than inheritance-heavy hierarchies.

items.title: Embedding
items.summary: Teach anonymous field embedding, promoted methods, and field shadowing without pretending embedding is inheritance.
objectives: Use embedding to promote behavior intentionally while keeping the underlying composed type visible
Explain what happens when outer fields or methods shadow embedded ones
items.production_relevance: Embedding is common in configuration, logging, and domain wrappers where reuse helps, but teams need to keep promotion readable and deliberate.

items.title: Bank Account Project
items.summary: Combine composition, embedding, promoted methods, and method shadowing in one bank-account milestone with runnable behavior and tests.
objectives: Build account types that reuse shared behavior through embedding instead of inheritance
Show the difference between promoted methods and intentionally shadowed behavior in one small domain
items.production_relevance: Banking-style account rules are a compact way to practice reuse boundaries, domain modeling, and method behavior overrides that show up in real service code.

items.title: Strings
items.summary: Introduce strings as immutable byte-backed values and show how packages like strings.Builder help build text efficiently.
objectives: Explain why Go strings are immutable and how that affects text manipulation
Choose standard-library string helpers instead of manual byte-by-byte reinvention
items.production_relevance: Text handling sits underneath config files, logs, payloads, and CLI output, so string fundamentals show up everywhere in Go systems.

items.title: Formatting
items.summary: Teach fmt verbs, width and precision controls, and when to format into strings versus directly to output.
objectives: Use the right fmt helper for inline formatting, string creation, and writer-based output
Explain how formatting choices affect readability and downstream text processing
items.production_relevance: Formatting verbs shape logs, CLI output, debug views, and text reports throughout production Go code.

items.title: Unicode and Runes
items.summary: Show the difference between bytes, runes, and UTF-8 iteration so learners stop assuming one byte always means one character.
objectives: Explain why byte indexing and rune iteration can produce different results
Use unicode helpers when text logic depends on characters instead of raw bytes
items.production_relevance: Unicode-aware text handling matters in APIs, CLI tools, validation, and internationalized user-facing systems.

items.title: Regex
items.summary: Introduce regexp compilation, match extraction, and structured capture groups for practical text parsing.
objectives: Compile and reuse regex patterns instead of rebuilding them inside loops
Extract structured data from text with capture groups instead of brittle manual slicing
items.production_relevance: Regex is common in parsing, validation, and log processing, but it must be used deliberately to stay readable and performant.

items.title: Text Templates
items.summary: Teach text/template as a safe way to render structured text output without manual string-concatenation sprawl.
objectives: Render repeated or conditional text layouts with template actions instead of brittle concatenation
Connect parsed data structures to template-driven output cleanly
items.production_relevance: Template-driven rendering shows up in config generation, emails, reports, and CLI summaries where output structure matters.

items.title: Config Parser Project
items.summary: Combine string handling, regex parsing, scanner-based reading, and template rendering into one config-parser milestone with runnable behavior and tests.
objectives: Parse key-value config data into a structured map using scanner and regex helpers
Render a stable summary from parsed data instead of printing ad hoc text line by line
items.production_relevance: Config parsing and text reporting are common in CLI tools, deployment helpers, and operational automation where predictable text handling matters.

items.title: Module Basics
items.summary: Explain what go.mod and go.sum do, how module paths identify code, and which commands keep module metadata healthy.
objectives: Explain the roles of go.mod and go.sum without treating them as mysterious generated files
Use core module commands like go mod tidy and go list to inspect and maintain dependency state
items.production_relevance: Every Go service, library, and CLI depends on module metadata being correct, reproducible, and understandable during local development and CI.

items.title: Managing Dependencies
items.summary: Show how to add, inspect, trim, and explain dependencies with the Go toolchain instead of editing module files by hand.
objectives: Use go get, go mod tidy, and go list intentionally when dependency state changes
Explain direct versus indirect dependencies and trace why a package is present
items.production_relevance: Dependency drift, unexpected indirect packages, and confused upgrade paths are normal engineering problems, so teams need repeatable module workflows.

items.title: Versioning Workshop
items.summary: Combine semantic versioning, the v2 import-path rule, and replace-directive reasoning in one runnable milestone with tests.
objectives: Model semantic versions and compare compatibility boundaries using the major-version rule
Explain why Go treats v2+ modules differently and when replace is useful during local development
items.production_relevance: Version boundaries and local overrides shape library upgrades, internal modules, and release safety in real Go codebases.

items.title: Build Tags
items.summary: Introduce conditional compilation and tagged tests for platform-specific code and optional integration surfaces.
objectives: Use //go:build constraints to separate platform-specific files cleanly
Explain why slow integration tests are often hidden behind opt-in tags
items.production_relevance: Build tags show up in cross-platform tools, optional integrations, and test suites that need fast defaults with explicit opt-in for heavy checks.

items.title: Args
items.summary: Introduce raw command-line arguments, environment variables, and basic exit-code behavior.
objectives: Read os.Args safely without indexing past the available arguments
Explain when to use environment variables versus command-line arguments
items.production_relevance: Small scripts, service entrypoints, and internal tools all need predictable command-line and environment handling.

items.title: Flags
items.summary: Teach typed option parsing with the standard flag package.
objectives: Register and parse typed flags with the standard library
Explain why flags are safer and clearer than ad hoc os.Args slicing for structured input
items.production_relevance: Typed flags are the foundation for internal tools, service bootstraps, and user-facing CLIs that need predictable options.

items.title: Subcommands
items.summary: Build multi-command CLIs with per-command flag sets instead of one giant flat argument surface.
objectives: Route subcommands explicitly through os.Args and switch logic
Use flag.NewFlagSet to keep command-specific flags isolated
items.production_relevance: Serious CLIs scale through subcommands, isolated flag scopes, and clear usage boundaries.

items.title: File Organizer
items.summary: Combine flags, directory reading, and safe file movement in one CLI milestone with a dry-run mode.
objectives: Build a flag-driven CLI that reads and organizes a directory by file extension
Use dry-run behavior to separate preview from destructive actions
items.production_relevance: Operational CLIs often combine filesystem work and command-line input, so safety features like dry-run are part of real engineering quality.

items.title: JSON Marshalling
items.summary: Introduce struct-to-JSON serialization and the struct tags that shape JSON output.
objectives: Marshal Go structs into JSON output with the standard library
Explain how struct tags affect field names and omission behavior
items.production_relevance: JSON serialization shows up in APIs, config generation, background jobs, and structured data exchange everywhere in Go.

items.title: JSON Unmarshalling
items.summary: Show how JSON input becomes Go data and why zero values can hide missing fields.
objectives: Decode JSON into structs and maps safely
Explain why missing JSON fields can silently become zero values
items.production_relevance: Unmarshal behavior shapes every API client, config loader, and structured-data ingestion path in Go.

items.title: JSON Encoder
items.summary: Teach stream-oriented JSON writing through json.NewEncoder and io.Writer surfaces.
objectives: Write JSON directly to io.Writer implementations with json.NewEncoder
Explain why streaming output is better than building giant byte slices in many cases
items.production_relevance: Streaming encoders are common in HTTP handlers, file exporters, and data pipelines that should not buffer everything in memory.

items.title: JSON Decoder
items.summary: Teach stream-oriented JSON reading with json.NewDecoder and reader-based workflows.
objectives: Decode JSON directly from io.Reader implementations
Handle EOF and repeated decode loops correctly for streamed data
items.production_relevance: Reader-based JSON decoding is the default production habit for HTTP bodies, files, and large payload streams.

items.title: Base64
items.summary: Explain binary-to-text encoding through base64 and where it fits relative to JSON.
objectives: Use standard and URL-safe base64 encodings appropriately
Explain why base64 is not encryption
items.production_relevance: Base64 appears in tokens, transport-safe payloads, inline assets, and protocol boundaries where raw binary is inconvenient.

items.title: Config Parser
items.summary: Combine file I/O, JSON decoding, and validation in one config-loading milestone with tests.
objectives: Load JSON config data from disk through json.NewDecoder
Validate decoded config structures and return useful wrapped errors
items.production_relevance: Configuration loading is one of the most common real-world uses of file I/O and decoding in Go services and tools.

items.title: Files
items.summary: Introduce basic file I/O, buffered reading, and file-handle lifecycle management.
objectives: Use os.ReadFile, os.WriteFile, and os.OpenFile appropriately
Explain why file handles must be closed promptly
items.production_relevance: File operations sit underneath config loaders, export tools, operational scripts, and data-processing jobs.

items.title: Paths
items.summary: Teach safe cross-platform path handling with the filepath package.
objectives: Use filepath helpers instead of manual path string construction
Explain why path normalization and absolute paths matter in tooling
items.production_relevance: Cross-platform tools live or die on path correctness, especially in CLIs and build tooling.

items.title: Directories
items.summary: Add directory creation, listing, traversal, and cleanup operations.
objectives: Create and inspect directories safely
Choose between single-level reads and recursive traversal intentionally
items.production_relevance: Directory traversal underpins search tools, file importers, deploy helpers, and backup workflows.

items.title: Temp Files
items.summary: Teach temporary files and directories as a disciplined pattern with immediate cleanup.
objectives: Create temporary directories and files safely with unique names
Clean up temporary resources immediately through defer
items.production_relevance: Temp resources show up in downloads, staging files, tests, and batch-processing pipelines.

items.title: Embed
items.summary: Introduce go:embed and embedded filesystem assets for single-binary delivery.
objectives: Embed files and directories directly into a Go binary
Explain when embedded assets are simpler than external runtime files
items.production_relevance: Embedding is common in CLIs, internal tools, and lightweight services that want one deployable binary.

items.title: I/O Patterns
items.summary: Explain io.Reader and io.Writer as the compositional core of Go I/O design.
objectives: Compose readers and writers with io.Copy and related helpers
Explain why interface-based I/O decouples code from storage and transport details
items.production_relevance: Nearly every production Go system depends on io.Reader and io.Writer composition across files, networks, and encoders.

items.title: Log Search
items.summary: Combine traversal, scanning, and filtering in one log-search milestone with tests.
objectives: Walk a directory tree and search matching files line by line
Keep search behavior resilient when some files cannot be read
items.production_relevance: Operational tooling often needs fast, memory-safe search across large sets of log or text files.

items.title: fs.FS Testing Seam
items.summary: Use fs.FS as a filesystem abstraction that works across real disk, embedded assets, and in-memory tests.
objectives: Accept fs.FS instead of raw paths when code needs a testable filesystem boundary
Explain how os.DirFS, embed.FS, and fstest.MapFS fit the same abstraction
items.production_relevance: fs.FS creates cleaner seams for tools and services that need filesystem logic without hard-coding real disk access into tests.

items.title: Connecting to SQLite
items.summary: Introduce blank imports, sql.Open, db.Ping, and the connection-pool model behind database/sql.
objectives: Explain why database drivers use blank imports for registration
Distinguish sql.Open setup from a real connectivity check with db.Ping
items.production_relevance: Database connectivity bugs often come from misunderstanding lazy connection setup and connection-pool behavior.

items.title: Executing Queries (INSERT)
items.summary: Use db.Exec with parameterized SQL and password hashing to write rows safely.
objectives: Insert rows with parameterized SQL instead of string-built queries
Explain why sensitive values should be transformed before storage
items.production_relevance: Safe write paths depend on parameterized SQL and deliberate data handling before values reach the database.

items.title: Select Queries
items.summary: Read single and multiple rows safely with QueryRow, Query, Scan, Close, and Err.
objectives: Map query results into Go structs with Scan
Explain why rows.Close and rows.Err are part of the read-side contract
items.production_relevance: Read-heavy services rely on predictable row scanning and resource cleanup to avoid pool exhaustion and subtle data bugs.

items.title: Prepared Statements
items.summary: Introduce explicit prepared statements and context-aware execution for more controlled database calls.
objectives: Explain when prepared statements are useful and when they add needless complexity
Use ExecContext to give database work a cancellation boundary
items.production_relevance: Prepared statements and context boundaries matter in services that issue repeated queries under latency and timeout constraints.

items.title: Transactions
items.summary: Show the all-or-nothing transaction pattern for multi-step writes and rollback safety.
objectives: Use BeginTx, Commit, and defer Rollback to keep multi-step writes consistent
Explain why slow non-database work should stay outside active transactions
items.production_relevance: Transactions protect data integrity, but only when they stay short, intentional, and limited to database work.

items.title: Repository Pattern Project
items.summary: Combine transactions, row mapping, and interface-driven data access in one SQLite-backed repository milestone.
objectives: Create a repository boundary that keeps application logic away from raw SQL details
Prove multi-table write and read flows through runnable behavior and tests
items.production_relevance: Repository boundaries and transaction-safe writes are common in services that need testable data access without hiding core database behavior.

items.title: Goroutines
items.summary: Introduce goroutines, scheduler basics, and the closure-capture bug that surprises new concurrent code.
objectives: Launch concurrent work with the go keyword
Explain why loop variables should be passed into goroutine closures explicitly
items.production_relevance: Every later Go concurrency pattern starts with goroutine lifecycle awareness and the cost of unmanaged background work.

items.title: WaitGroups
items.summary: Teach the standard Add, Done, and Wait coordination pattern for concurrent tasks.
objectives: Use sync.WaitGroup to wait for a fixed set of goroutines
Explain why WaitGroup values should be passed by pointer
items.production_relevance: Services often fan out work across goroutines and still need one explicit boundary before the request can finish.

items.title: Channels
items.summary: Introduce typed communication channels and the blocking behavior that synchronizes concurrent work.
objectives: Send and receive values across channels safely
Explain the difference between synchronization and buffering
items.production_relevance: Channels are the default coordination tool when goroutines need to hand work or results to each other without shared mutable state.

items.title: Buffered Channels
items.summary: Explain buffered channel capacity, bounded queues, and when asynchronous sends still block.
objectives: Use buffered channels to decouple producers and consumers
Explain when buffered channels block on full or empty buffers
items.production_relevance: Bounded channel buffers are a common backpressure tool when one stage of a pipeline is faster than another.

items.title: Closing Channels
items.summary: Show how channel closure signals completion and why only senders should close channels.
objectives: Use range and comma-ok patterns with closed channels
Explain why receivers should not close channels they do not own
items.production_relevance: Producer shutdown, worker drain logic, and broadcast completion all rely on clear channel ownership rules.

items.title: Pipeline Project
items.summary: Combine channel stages and cancellation into a small pipeline-shaped project.
objectives: Coordinate multiple stages of work through channels
Use explicit cancellation boundaries to stop background work cleanly
items.production_relevance: Real concurrent systems often look like pipelines with bounded stages, cancellation signals, and explicit hand-off points.

items.title: Concurrent Downloader
items.summary: Combine fan-out, result aggregation, and bounded concurrency in one downloader milestone.
objectives: Coordinate one goroutine per download while keeping concurrency bounded
Aggregate download results without shared global state
items.production_relevance: Network-heavy tools need concurrency limits, explicit result aggregation, and graceful failure handling instead of unlimited fan-out.

items.title: Background and TODO
items.summary: Introduce root contexts and the core methods that every context implementation exposes.
objectives: Distinguish context.Background from context.TODO
Explain what Deadline, Done, Err, and Value represent
items.production_relevance: Context is the standard control surface for I/O cancellation and scoped metadata in production Go code.

items.title: WithCancel
items.summary: Show how cancellation signals propagate through context trees.
objectives: Create and cancel child contexts manually
Explain how ctx.Done helps stop background work
items.production_relevance: Cancellation propagation prevents leaked goroutines and wasted I/O work after the caller no longer cares about the result.

items.title: WithTimeout and WithDeadline
items.summary: Teach deadline-based cancellation for bounded work and outbound calls.
objectives: Use context.WithTimeout and context.WithDeadline correctly
Recognize context.DeadlineExceeded as the timeout signal
items.production_relevance: Timeouts are the production guardrail that keeps one slow downstream dependency from holding everything hostage.

items.title: WithValue
items.summary: Explain request-scoped metadata and the narrow role of context values.
objectives: Store request metadata with custom key types safely
Explain why context values should not replace normal dependencies
items.production_relevance: Trace IDs, request IDs, and auth metadata often need to flow with a request without turning function signatures into global bags of state.

items.title: Timeout-Aware API Client
items.summary: Apply timeout-bound contexts to outbound HTTP requests and surface useful failure behavior.
objectives: Attach a timeout-bound context directly to an HTTP request
Recognize and report timeout failures clearly
items.production_relevance: Outbound HTTP clients need cancellation boundaries or they become a common source of stuck goroutines and cascading failures.

items.title: Time Basics
items.summary: Introduce time values, durations, and elapsed-time calculations.
objectives: Create and compare time.Time values safely
Use time.Duration for sleep and elapsed-time calculations
items.production_relevance: Correct time math underpins retries, deadlines, metrics, and any real program that measures work over time.

items.title: Time Formatting
items.summary: Teach parsing and formatting with Go's reference-time layout model.
objectives: Format time values with standard layouts and custom layouts
Parse time strings without guessing locale-specific formats
items.production_relevance: APIs, logs, and schedulers all depend on predictable parse and format behavior across systems.

items.title: Timers and Tickers
items.summary: Explain one-shot and repeating timed events plus the cleanup rules that keep them safe.
objectives: Use time.Timer and time.Ticker for one-shot and repeating events
Explain why ticker.Stop is part of the contract
items.production_relevance: Timers and tickers are the foundation for retries, timeouts, reminders, and recurring background work.

items.title: Console Reminder
items.summary: Combine timers, tickers, and command-line input in one small reminder milestone.
objectives: Schedule a one-shot reminder with time.AfterFunc
Drive a countdown loop with time.NewTicker and explicit cleanup
items.production_relevance: Timer-driven programs need the same lifecycle care as larger services: explicit cleanup, bounded loops, and predictable exit behavior.

items.title: errgroup Basics
items.summary: Introduce errgroup as the safer coordination tool when concurrent work can fail.
objectives: Use errgroup.Group to launch concurrent work that can return errors
Explain how errgroup differs from a bare sync.WaitGroup
items.production_relevance: Concurrent service startup and fan-out flows often need one coordination point that can surface failure instead of silently discarding it.

items.title: errgroup with Context Cancellation
items.summary: Teach automatic sibling cancellation with errgroup.WithContext in fan-out workflows.
objectives: Use errgroup.WithContext to stop sibling goroutines on the first failure
Explain how context cancellation propagates through the worker pipeline
items.production_relevance: Service fan-out paths need automatic stop signals or they keep wasting work after a failure already decided the outcome.

items.title: sync.Pool
items.summary: Introduce sync.Pool for short-lived object reuse and the reset discipline it requires.
objectives: Use Get, Reset, and Put safely with temporary pooled objects
Explain why sync.Pool is for measured allocation pressure, not default design
items.production_relevance: Pools help reduce allocation churn in hot paths, but only when used deliberately and reset correctly to avoid stale-data bugs.

items.title: Bounded Pipeline Exercise
items.summary: Combine errgroup, cancellation, SetLimit, and sync.Pool in one bounded-concurrency exercise.
objectives: Build a bounded concurrent pipeline that stops on the first failure
Reuse large temporary buffers instead of allocating one per work item
items.production_relevance: Bounded fan-out plus buffer reuse is a common production pattern when throughput matters more than launching unlimited parallel work.

items.title: URL Health Checker
items.summary: Build a latency-aware URL checker with bounded fan-out and pooled clients.
objectives: Run concurrent HTTP checks with errgroup and bounded fan-out
Collect stable, latency-sorted results without losing per-request detail
items.production_relevance: Health checks and outbound service probes are a real concurrency workload: they need limits, cancellation, and predictable result handling.

items.title: Unit Testing
items.summary: Introduce Go's built-in testing package through small focused unit tests.
objectives: Write tests with testing.T and standard Go test file conventions
Explain why simple tests should stay close to the code they verify
items.production_relevance: Reliable services depend on small fast tests that catch regressions before review and deployment.

items.title: Table-Driven Tests
items.summary: Teach the idiomatic Go pattern for structured test case tables and sub-tests.
objectives: Use a test-case table to cover multiple inputs without copy-pasting test functions
Use t.Run to isolate failures per case
items.production_relevance: Table-driven tests scale better than one-off assertions when behavior has many input variations.

items.title: HTTP Handler Testing
items.summary: Show how to test handlers with httptest instead of starting a real HTTP server.
objectives: Use httptest.NewRecorder and httptest.NewRequest to exercise handler behavior
Explain why handler tests should stay server-free at the unit level
items.production_relevance: Handler tests give fast confidence on status codes, bodies, and method handling without bringing network flakiness into the feedback loop.

items.title: Benchmarking
items.summary: Introduce testing.B, sub-benchmarks, and allocation-aware benchmark interpretation.
objectives: Write benchmarks with testing.B and b.ReportAllocs
Explain how setup and allocation noise can distort benchmark conclusions
items.production_relevance: Benchmarks let engineers validate performance claims before and after a change instead of arguing from intuition.

items.title: CPU Profile
items.summary: Teach offline CPU profiling with runtime/pprof and the before-profile-after workflow.
objectives: Capture a CPU profile and inspect it with go tool pprof
Explain the difference between flat and cumulative time
items.production_relevance: CPU profiles reveal hot paths and expensive functions that benchmarks alone cannot prioritize in a whole program.

items.title: Live pprof Endpoint
items.summary: Expose a live pprof endpoint on an internal-only port and inspect a running service under load.
objectives: Expose live pprof endpoints safely on a separate internal port
Use live CPU and heap endpoints to inspect a running service
items.production_relevance: Live pprof is how teams investigate CPU, heap, goroutine, and mutex behavior in production-like conditions without guessing.

items.title: Naming Conventions
items.summary: Teach the package naming rules that keep Go APIs readable and domain-focused.
objectives: Explain how package names compose with exported symbols
Spot vague package names like utils and helpers before they spread
items.production_relevance: Package names become part of every import path and every exported API, so bad names create long-term friction across the codebase.

items.title: Visibility and Export Rules
items.summary: Use export rules and the internal directory to tighten package boundaries.
objectives: Explain how exported and unexported identifiers shape the public API
Describe when internal packages are the right boundary tool
items.production_relevance: Visibility discipline keeps internal details from leaking into call sites and lets packages change safely over time.

items.title: Project Layout
items.summary: Choose a Go project layout that matches the real size and responsibilities of the system.
objectives: Explain when a flat project layout is enough and when cmd or internal are justified
Call out layout anti-patterns before they turn into long-term structure debt
items.production_relevance: Project layout decisions affect testability, import boundaries, discoverability, and how safely a codebase can grow.

items.title: slog Basics
items.summary: Introduce Go's structured logging model with handlers, attributes, levels, and groups.
objectives: Explain why structured logs are more useful than ad hoc strings
Use TextHandler and JSONHandler with typed attributes
items.production_relevance: Structured logging is the baseline observability surface for production services and incident response.

items.title: Context-Keyed Logger
items.summary: Carry request-scoped logging fields through handlers and middleware with context.
objectives: Inject loggers into request context safely
Explain why request correlation depends on context propagation
items.production_relevance: Request-scoped logs are how teams reconstruct what happened inside one request without grepping unrelated traffic.

items.title: Custom slog Handler
items.summary: Implement the slog.Handler interface to understand how structured logging backends plug in.
objectives: Explain the slog.Handler contract and its concurrency requirements
Build a custom handler that changes how logs are rendered or routed
items.production_relevance: Handler boundaries are where teams integrate logging with external sinks, formatting rules, and redaction pipelines.

items.title: zerolog Comparison
items.summary: Compare slog with zerolog to show when performance pressure justifies a different logging stack.
objectives: Explain the allocation trade-offs between slog and zerolog
Describe when a logging dependency change is justified by measurement
items.production_relevance: Teams should choose logging libraries based on measured hot-path behavior, not on style preferences alone.

items.title: PII Redactor
items.summary: Apply the track by redacting sensitive fields inside the structured logging pipeline.
objectives: Use ReplaceAttr to centralize redaction logic for sensitive attributes
Keep redaction out of individual logging call sites
items.production_relevance: Sensitive data should be protected by the logging layer itself so a missed call site does not leak secrets into logs.

items.title: signal NotifyContext
items.summary: Introduce signal.NotifyContext and signal-aware worker cancellation for production processes.
objectives: Explain how SIGINT and SIGTERM should influence Go process behavior
Use NotifyContext to coordinate shutdown across goroutines
items.production_relevance: Production binaries are expected to react predictably to termination signals during deploys, restarts, and operator actions.

items.title: HTTP Graceful Drain
items.summary: Drain in-flight HTTP requests with server.Shutdown instead of dropping traffic during deploys.
objectives: Explain what http.Server.Shutdown actually does for active and idle connections
Describe why graceful drain matters during rolling deploys
items.production_relevance: HTTP graceful shutdown prevents deployment-time request loss and turns termination into a controlled part of service behavior.

items.title: Shutdown Capstone
items.summary: Coordinate readiness, HTTP drain, worker drain, and final cleanup in one production-style shutdown flow.
objectives: Explain the shutdown dependency order across readiness, HTTP, workers, and shared resources
Use errgroup to coordinate a multi-component shutdown sequence
items.production_relevance: Complex services need one coherent shutdown plan, not a pile of independent cleanup calls that race each other during deploys.
